key_insight: AI is shifting nanoparticle electron microscopy from image interpretation to scientific inference by extracting quantitative structure, dynamics, and property-linked insights from complex TEM/STEM datasets.
engineering_relevance: These methods can accelerate materials discovery by automating nanoparticle characterization, improving defect and morphology analysis, and enabling closed-loop microscopy and autonomous experimentation.
method: Conventional ML, CNNs, and transformer-based models for detection, segmentation, restoration, and defect identification
Self-supervised, foundation, multimodal, and physics-informed learning for robust inference across microscopy and metadata
Integration with simulations and in situ TEM analysis to connect structure, dynamics, synthesis, and function
evidence_signal: The review compares AI methods across TEM, HRTEM, STEM, and in situ TEM, emphasizing benchmarking, data requirements, advantages, and limitations rather than reporting a single new dataset.
watch_item: Performance can be limited by sparse labeled data, domain shift across microscopes/materials, and the need for careful benchmarking before autonomous deployment.
paper_title: The evolution of AI from image interpretation toward scientific inference in nanoparticle electron microscopy
